hook.scenario: You put Rs10,000 in a new neo-bank app offering 12% returns. Do they calculate it simply at year-end, or compounded daily? The difference is whether you can buy a new phone or just a cover.

mental_model.intuition: Simple Interest is linear growth. Compound Interest is exponential growth (interest earning its own interest).
mental_model.description: SI adds the exact same amount every cycle. CI multiplies by a constant scaling factor every cycle, making it just successive percentage increases applied to the growing total.

pattern_discovery.setup: Track interest earned each year on Rs1000 at 10% for both SI and CI.
cases: Year 2: SI=100, CI=110 (CI earns interest on last year's interest too)
Year 3: SI=100, CI=121 (CI keeps growing; SI stays flat)
pattern_discovery.prompt: Why does CI pull ahead of SI after year 1, and by how much exactly?
pattern_discovery.rule: CI for any year is the previous year's CI scaled by (1 + R/100). The difference between CI and SI grows each year because CI reinvests the interest.

formula.statement: SI = P x R x T / 100. Amount under SI = P + SI = P x (1 + RT/100). CI Amount = P x (1 + R/100)^T. CI = Amount - P. Compounded half-yearly: A = P x (1 + R/200)^(2T). Compounded quarterly: A = P x (1 + R/400)^(4T). Difference (CI - SI) for 2 years = P x (R/100)^2. Difference (CI - SI) for 3 years = P x (R/100)^2 x (R/100 + 3).
formula.explain: When compounding frequency increases (monthly, quarterly, half-yearly), halve or quarter the rate and multiply the time periods accordingly. The 2-year difference shortcut P(R/100)^2 is the most-tested formula after the basic ones.

worked_example.problem: Find the compound interest on Rs5,000 at 10% per annum for 2 years, compounded annually. Also find the SI for the same terms and the difference.
steps: Sanity-check using shortcut: Diff = P x (R/100)^2 = 5000 x (0.1)^2 = 5000 x 0.01 = 50. Matches.
worked_example.answer: CI = Rs1050, SI = Rs1000, Difference = Rs50.

worked_example.problem: Rs8,000 is invested at 10% per annum compounded half-yearly. Find the amount after 1 year.
steps: Half-yearly: halve the rate (10/2 = 5%) and double the time periods (1 year = 2 half-years).
Compare with annual compounding: 8000 x 1.10 = 8800. Half-yearly gives more (8820 > 8800). Makes sense.

shortcuts.title: 2-Year CI-SI Difference
shortcuts.example: Diff on Rs1000 at 10% for 2 years = 1000 x (10/100)^2 = Rs10

shortcuts.title: Rule of 72
shortcuts.trick: Years to double your money = 72 / R
shortcuts.example: At 12% CI, your money doubles in roughly 72/12 = 6 years.

common_mistakes.title: Monthly vs Annual Rate
common_mistakes.explanation: If interest is compounded half-yearly, halve the rate and double the time periods. If quarterly, use R/4 and 4T. Do not use the annual rate blindly.

recall_questions.q: What is the 2-year difference between CI and SI on Rs5000 at 5%?
If money doubles in 4 years at SI, what is the rate?
recall_questions.answer: Interest = Principal -> P = P x R x 4 / 100 -> R = 25%

recall_questions.q: Does SI ever exceed CI for the same rate and positive time?
recall_questions.answer: No, for time > 1 year, CI > SI. For time = 1 year with annual compounding, CI = SI.

oa_questions.question: A sum of money doubles itself in 5 years at simple interest. In how many years will it become 4 times itself?
oa_questions.approach: SI = P takes 5 years (rate = 20%). To become 4P, SI = 3P. Since SI is linear, 3P takes 3 x 5 = 15 years.

oa_questions.question: The difference between CI and SI on a sum for 2 years at 8% is Rs32. What is the sum?
